coredata.dc:title: State-of-charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on incommensurate fractional-order observer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Communications in Nonlinear Science and Numerical Simulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Liping
Guo, Wenliang
Lopes, António M.
Wu, Ranchao
Li, Penghua
Yin, Lisheng
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a new state-of-charge (SOC) estimation method for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) based on incommensurate fractional-order (FO) observer. First, a FO RC equivalent circuit model is constructed to describe the charging and discharging dynamic characteristics of the LIB. An adaptive FO particle swarm optimization (AFOPSO) algorithm is employed to identify the parameters of the established FO model. The accuracy of the parameterized model is verified, showing its superiority over the available ones. Then, by using the comparison principle of FO systems with multi-order and stability of FO incommensurate linear systems with multi-order, a new order-dependent incommensurate FO observer is designed for battery SOC estimation. Finally, the validity and feasibility of the proposed SOC observer are examined with real time experimental data of LIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SOC estimation
FO equivalent circuit
Observer

head.ce:title: State-of-charge estimation for lithium-ion batteries based on incommensurate fractional-order observer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Hefei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Automotive Electronics Engineering Research Center, College of Automation, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Automotive Electronics Engineering Research Center, College of Automation, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Automotive Electronics Engineering Research Center, College of Automation, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a new state-of-charge (SOC) estimation method for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) based on incommensurate fractional-order (FO) observer. First, a FO RC equivalent circuit model is constructed to describe the charging and discharging dynamic characteristics of the LIB. An adaptive FO particle swarm optimization (AFOPSO) algorithm is employed to identify the parameters of the established FO model. The accuracy of the parameterized model is verified, showing its superiority over the available ones. Then, by using the comparison principle of FO systems with multi-order and stability of FO incommensurate linear systems with multi-order, a new order-dependent incommensurate FO observer is designed for battery SOC estimation. Finally, the validity and feasibility of the proposed SOC observer are examined with real time experimental data of LIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data is available online. The link is given.

ce:section.ce:para: Promoting the development of hybrid or pure electric energy vehicles is one of the main strategic measures to alleviate problems related to environmental pollution, scarcity of oil resources and limited use of new energy solutions [1,2]. Due to their high energy density, low discharge rate and high cycle times, LIBs have become one of the core components of the power system of new energy vehicles. However, LIBs safety and power performance are affected by many factors, such as temperature and poor SOC, state of health (SOH) and state of power (SOP) estimation methods. Accurate SOC estimation supports available capacity monitoring and related state feedback control, which are particularly important for maintaining safe and efficient operation of LIBs [3,4].

Due to the complex chemical reactions in LIBs and their nonlinear and strong couplings, accurate SOC estimation has always been challenging. At present, there are many SOC estimation methods, namely model-free and model-based alternatives. Model-free methods include the ampere-hour integration [5], open-circuit voltage and electrical impedance [6] methods. These are characterized by having low complexity and being easy to implement on a microprocessor. However, because there is no feedback-based adjustment of the estimation error, they are essentially open-loop and their accuracy is affected by the drift of the initial value and accumulated error. Model-based methods are mainly divided into two types, that is, the ones based on electrochemical model and those based on equivalent circuit model. Although the electrochemical model based on nonlinear coupled partial differential equations has high accuracy, it has obvious problems, such as complex model structure, difficult parameter identification and low running speed. It is not suitable for hybrid electric vehicles and pure electric vehicles. The equivalent circuit model captures the dynamic characteristics of LIBs by simulating the output–output behavior of electrical components, and uses resistive and capacitive elements [7]. Since it has simple structure, few parameters, high accuracy and can be implemented easily, it is widely used in the state estimation of LIBs. The estimation methods based on equivalent circuit model include the Kalman filter and their variations, such as the extended Kalman filter (EKF), Sigma-Point Kalman Filter (SPKF) and unscented Kalman filter (UKF) [8–10], and state observer, such as the Luenberger,
H
∞
and PI observers [11–14].

However, the ideal capacitance and resistance elements in the equivalent circuit model cannot accurately describe the dynamic process of the battery [15]. Improving the model accuracy by augmenting the number of RC links increases the complexity of the model structure and the number of parameters to be identified. Therefore, in order to improve the equivalent circuit model and better capture the dynamic characteristics of the battery without complicating the model structure, the FO constant phase angle element (CPE) was introduced, and the potential advantages of the FO model to accurately represent the battery dynamics were discussed [16]. As for LIBs, the FO models were proven to capture the system characteristics at high fidelity, while keeping reasonable calculation requirements [17–19]. In recent years, FO models have been widely used in battery state estimation and reliability analysis [20–25].

Compared with the Kalman filter, the method based on state observer has the advantages of simplicity, high efficiency, robustness and accuracy. The observer based method estimates the SOC by using the characteristics of a switching function, while reducing the impact of model uncertainty, measurement noise and external interference [26]. The
H
∞
, Luenberger and adaptive observers are widely used to estimate SOC based on integer-order models of the equivalent circuit of LIBs [27,28]. However, because the convergence of the estimation error of FO systems is not easy to prove, it is difficult to estimate the SOC of FO equivalent circuit models of LIBs by designing an observer. A nonlinear FO observer combining a Luenberger and a sliding mode terms was designed in [29]. In order to overcome the parameter uncertainty, and the modeling and measurement errors, a FO sliding mode observer to estimate SOC was proposed in [30]. A FO Luenberger observer was designed in [31], and the stability of the system was proven through the continuous frequency distribution transform method; but this kind of transform method lacks strict proof (the real state converges to zero and the pseudo-state also converges to zero) and an impulse function is used in the initial value configuration defined by Caputo.

It should be pointed out that all the FO observers mentioned previously are independent of the order of the system. However, as pointed out in [32], considering the order of the system is relevant in many aspects, such as in the estimation of the SOC, the determination of aging, and the assessment of the discharge rate of LIBs. The existing work on FO model-based estimation of the SOC of LIBs reveals some issues:
(1) Commensurate FO elements are used, but the differences between different FO elements are neglected;

(2) Relationships between the order of FO elements and the SOC are ignored, and the observers are designed independently of the order of the system, thus, not capturing the FO characteristics of LIBs;
(3) Theoretical guarantees of stability and robustness for the incommensurate FO system are not provided.

To the best of the authors knowledge, the derivation of FO observers that are related with the order of the system for SOC estimation has not been explored. Therefore, to take complete advantage of the observer and reduce its conservativeness, a new order-dependent incommensurate FO observer is proposed in this paper. The novel contributions are:

(1) A FO equivalent circuit model is established to capture the dynamic characteristics of charge transfer and double-layer effect inside the LIB. An improved AFOPSO algorithm is proposed to identify the parameters of the established FO model and the accuracy of that model is verified.

(2) A new order-dependent incommensurate FO observer is designed, where the comparison principle of FO systems and the stability result of linear incommensurate FO systems are employed to show the observation error system stable.
(3) The proposed incommensurate FO observer is related to the order of the FO equivalent circuit, which has reduced conservativeness and reflects well the FO characteristics of the battery.

The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 the main concepts of FO calculus and lemmas that are used in the paper are introduced. Also, the FO RC equivalent circuit model of the LIB, the parameter identification, and the accuracy of the parameterized battery model are analyzed. In Section 3 the incommensurate FO observer is designed. In Section 4 the effectiveness of the proposed method is discussed via several experiments at different temperatures. In Section 5 the main conclusions are outlined.

Calculus is a common mathematical basis adopted for the description of dynamical systems, which has been widely used in scientific research. The generalization from classic toward FO calculus was put forward by Leibnitz, as early as
1695
. In recent decades, it has been gradually realized that any system is not of strict integer-order (IO). In particular, physical system with diffusion dynamics, hysteresis characteristics, hydrodynamics, and others, are very suitable to be described by FO models with high accuracy. Batteries are an example of such practical systems, where FO calculus can be used to describe their internal electrochemical dynamic characteristics.

Usually, the operator
t
0
D
t
α
is adopted to represent the derivative or integral of arbitrary order α with respect to t , in which
t
0
is the initial time. Herein,
t
0
is considered equal to 0 . When
α > 0
,
t
0
D
t
α
represents the fractional derivative, while for
α < 0
, it stands for the fractional integral. Throughout this paper, we will consider only the fractional derivatives (
α > 0
), and the notation will be then simplified to
D
α
.

The Grünwald–Letnikov (GL) is one of the most commonly used definitions [33]. The α th order GL fractional derivative of a function
x
( t )
is given by:
(1)
D
α
x
( t )
=
lim
Δ T → 0
1

represents the Newton binomial coefficient generalized to real numbers, expressed as:

Γ

,

represents the Gamma function given by:
(3)

∫
0

ξ
α − 1
e
− ξ
d ξ .
The Gamma function plays a very important role in fractional calculus, which is a generalization of the factorial operator for real numbers.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Lemma 2.1
ce:enunciation.ce:section-title: [34] Comparison Principle of FO Systems with Multi-Order

ce:para.ce:italic: Consider the following FO differential inequalities with initial conditions
, it is stable if the characteristic equation

ce:section.ce:para:
A model that can accurately capture the dynamic characteristics of LIBs is very important for SOC estimation. The electrochemical impedance spectrogram (EIS) provides impedance values at different frequencies, and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy test can accurately reflect the dynamic response of the battery [36]. For a typical LIB, the Nyquist plot of its impedance is as shown in Fig. 1. The spectrum includes three main parts: (1) a low frequency band in which diffusion of solid-phase lithium-ions in the LIB electrodes occurs, (2) a middle frequency band due to charge transfer reaction and electrochemical double layer effect, and (3) a high frequency band due to Ohmic resistance of inductive components. An IO equivalent circuit model uses
R C
links to capture the charge transfer and double-layer effect in the mid-range band. However, the electrochemical impedance spectrum of ideal capacitor elements should be a semicircle, which is inconsistent with the observed ellipse in the middle-range band. So, IO models cannot accurately describe the dynamic characteristics of the battery. In this way, many works have proposed using CPEs, instead of ideal capacitors, to establish a FO model that better describes the internal characteristics of LIBs. The impedance of a CPE is expressed as follows:
(4)
Z
( s )
=
1
C
s
α
.
where C represents the capacitance value of the CPE element and α denotes its FO.

The fractional model we built is shown in Fig. 2, where
U
o c
stands for the open circuit voltage,
R
0
denotes the ohmic internal resistance,
R
1
is the electrochemical polarization resistance, CPE stands for the fractional capacitors,
V
0
represents the terminal voltage of the battery, and I corresponds to the load current. If we denote by
V
1
the voltages on the two parallel associations, then the dynamic equations can be expressed as:
(5)

,

is the nominal capacity of the LIB and η is the Coulomb efficiency. The SOC of the battery can be written as:

The main current and voltage data of the battery INR 18650-20R with a capacity 2000 mAH are provided by the CALCE Battery Research Group [37]. We use four typical current and voltage test datasets at three different temperatures (
0
° C , 25
° C , 45
° C
) of the vehicle operating conditions: Dynamic Stress Test (DST), Federal Urban Driving Schedule (FUDS), Beijing Dynamic Stress Test (BJDST) and Highway Driving Schedule (US06). The current and voltage data under the DST conditions at
25
° C
, as shown in Figs. 3 and 4, are used to identify the parameters of the model. The current and voltage data for the other three working conditions at different temperatures are used to verify the accuracy of the SOC estimation algorithm.

Particle swarm optimization algorithms (PSO) originated from the study of bird predation behavior. The advantage of PSO algorithms is that they are simple and easy to implement without many parameter adjustments. However, the traditional PSO algorithms have slow convergence speeds and can easily fall into local optimal solutions, which leads to large errors in the identification results. The FO particle swarm optimization algorithm (FOPSO) is a novel technique for controlling the convergence rate of a PSO algorithm that uses the fractional calculus (FC) concepts [38]. It was shown that FC directly influences the speed of convergence of the PSO. Furthermore, in order to avoid the PSO algorithm to fall into local optima and to improve search efficiency, we propose an AFOPSO to identify the LIB model parameters. As such, the FO velocity adaptation law becomes:
(18)

α

ν
i
k
)

2
(
G

i
k
)
,
χ
i
k + 1
=
χ
i
k
+
ν
i
k + 1
,
where
ν
i
is the particle velocity,
χ
i
denotes the particle position,
P
b e s
t
i
represents the individual optimal value of the particles,
G
b e s
t
i
stands for the global optimal value of the particles, and α is the update rate of FO. Then, using GL derivative given above, one has:

.

The first
r = 4
terms of the discrete-time FO derivative, that is the truncated GL derivative, were used in [38]. Then, based on the FOPSO, we add and improve the inertia weight of the algorithm. The magnitude of the inertia weight is related to its distance from the global optimum, that is, the weight ω can dynamically change according to the different positions of the particles. The AFOPSO and weight coefficient formula is as follows:
(20)

where f represents the real-time objective function value of particles, and
f

and
f
m i n
represent the average and the minimum target value of all particles at present, respectively. It can be seen from the above formula that the inertia weight changes with the change of the value of the particle objective function.

Here, we set as goal the minimization of the root mean square error (RMSE) between the measured and the estimated voltages. Let us define the objective function f as:
(21)
min f =
∑
k = 1
N
[
U
t
( k )
−
U
̂
t
( k )
]
2
,
where
U
t
( k )
and
U
̂
t
( k )
are the measured and the estimated voltages, and N is the number of the sampling points. The algorithm flow chart is as follows (see Table 1):

Tables 2 and 3 show the results of the parameter identification of the FO model using the AFOPSO algorithm. Fig. 5 shows the nonlinear relationship between open circuit voltage (OCV) and SOC fitted by a 6th-order polynomial based on low current OCV test data. In order to verify the accuracy of the model and the effectiveness of the AFOPSO algorithm, we compare the identification results of the AFOPSO, FOPSO and PSO, which are shown in Figs. 6 and 7. From Fig. 6, one can see that the FO model is more accurate than the IO model, and the parameters identified by the AFOPSO are better than the ones identified by both the FOPSO and PSO. From Fig. 7, we verify that the RMSE of the FO model identified by the AFOPSO, the FO model identified by the FOPSO and PSO, and the IO model identified by the PSO are 9.87 mV, 11.64 mV, 19.33 mV and 41.67 mV, respectively. Therefore, the identified parameters have good accuracy and the proposed model and identification algorithm used herein are reliable.

In this section, an incommensurate FO observer is designed. The comparison principle of FO systems with multi-order and the stability of linear incommensurate FO systems are used to derive the observer gain.

Considering the FO battery model defined by (9)–(13), the following observer is proposed:
(22)
D
α
x
̂
( t )
= A
x
̂
( t )
+ B u
( t )
+ L
[ y
( t )
−
y
̂
( t )
]
,
y

It follows from condition (24) that

and

. Therefore, based on Lemma 2.2, the corresponding comparative system (26) or (27) is stable. Furthermore, from Lemma 2.1,
V
( t )
< W
( t )
, that is,
V =
x
˜
( t )
T
x
˜
( t )
→ 0
,
x
˜
( t )
→ 0
. So, the observer error system (23) is also stable. This completes the proof.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark 3.1
ce:enunciation.ce:para: Note that the designed observer is related to the order of the FO system, while the observer designed in [29,31] is independent of the order of the system.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark 3.2
ce:enunciation.ce:para:
Generally speaking, the FO
2 − R C
equivalent circuit is better then the
1 − R C
equivalent circuit in terms of modeling precision. Due to the lack of stability of linear FO incommensurate systems with dimension greater than 2, to consider the stability of incommensurate FO nonlinear systems remains formidable. Therefore, to construct an incommensurate FO observer for a FO
2 − R C
equivalent circuit of LIBs is still challenging.

ce:section.ce:para:
In the simulations, the lower bound of the Lipschitz constant
m = 2 . 12
can be calculated based on (8) and (9). We chose
m = 2 . 51 ,
L
1
= 1 . 33 × 1
0
− 4
and
L
2
= 2 . 03 × 1
0
− 4
, which satisfy the condition (24).

Figs. 8–10 depict the voltage and current data of the BJDST, FUDS and US06 working conditions at
25
° C
, respectively. In order to verify the effectiveness of the proposed estimation algorithm, we compare its estimation accuracy and running time with the EKF and the FO EKF (FEKF) under three different working conditions and different temperatures. The algorithms were executed in MATLAB
2018
b on a computer with a 3.2 GHz processor,
16
GB memory and
64
-bit operating system.

Figs. 11–13 show the estimation results and errors of the proposed algorithm, the EKF and the FEKF under three different working conditions at
25
° C
. It can be seen from the main figure and from the partial enlarged plot that the proposed algorithm yields results closer to the reference and that the estimation error is smaller. Tables 4–6 show the maximum error, RMSE, mean square error (MSE) and calculation time of the three estimation algorithms. We verify that the estimation error and calculation time of the proposed algorithm are smaller than those of the EKF and FEKF algorithms under three different working conditions. The RMSE of the proposed algorithm can be stable below 1% under the different driving conditions at
25
° C
.

The LIB is a complex nonlinear and strong coupling system, and its internal state is easily influenced by temperature, which affects the accuracy of the model and SOC estimation. In order to verify the robustness of the proposed algorithm, we also compare the SOC estimation of the proposed, the EKF and the FEKF algorithms for three different working conditions at
0
° C
and
45
° C
.

Figs. 14–16 illustrate the estimation results and errors between the proposed algorithm, the EKF and the FEKF, under three different driving conditions at
0
° C
. It can be seen that the estimation yielded by the algorithm proposed herein is obviously better than those of the EKF and the FEKF, and its estimation accuracy is less affected by temperature change than the EKF. The accuracy of the FEKF estimation based on the FO model is obviously better than that of the EKF based on the IO model. Due to the temperature change, the convergence and estimation accuracy of the EKF and FEKF algorithms are obviously reduced. Tables 7–9 show the estimation results. We verify that the maximum estimation error of the EKF is 6.16% and the stability of the EKF estimation algorithm is obviously deteriorated due to the influence of temperature. The maximum error of our algorithm is always within 2.7%. In addition, the calculation time of the FEKF is the longest. Conversely, the calculation time of the proposed algorithm is obviously lower than those of the EKF and the FEKF. This all proves that the algorithm proposed in this paper has good accuracy and reduced computational complexity.

Figs. 17–19 show the estimation results and errors of the proposed algorithm, the EKF and the FEKF at
45
° C
. It can be seen that the proposed algorithm can still maintain good estimation accuracy even when the temperature rises. Our algorithm is robust to the decrease of estimation accuracy caused by the change on the system parameters due to temperature variations. Moreover, its estimation results, summarized in Tables 10–12, show its superiority when compared with the traditional EKF algorithm. At the same time, compared with the FEKF algorithm, the calculation time is shorter and the computational complexity is smaller. Due to the change of temperature, the estimation error of the EKF algorithm has increased to more than 3%, while the error of the proposed algorithm can still remain within 1%.

An incommensurate FO observer was proposed to accurately estimate the SOC of LIBs at different temperatures and operating conditions. An FO equivalent circuit model was established to capture the battery dynamic characteristics. An AFOPSO algorithm was employed to identify the parameters of the FO model and its accuracy was verified. The SOC estimation results of the designed observer were compared with the traditional EKF and FEKF algorithms under three different driving conditions and three distinct temperatures. The simulation results showed that the proposed method is superior to both the EKF and FEKF in terms of SOC estimation accuracy and calculation time, under different working conditions.

Liping Chen: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Wenliang Guo: Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft. António M. Lopes: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Ranchao Wu: Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Penghua Li: Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lisheng Yin: Software, Methodology, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by National Natural Science Funds of China (Nos. 62073114; 11971032; 52272388), Chongqing Outstanding Youth Fund Project (cstc2021jcyj-jqX0001) and Science and Technology Research Project of Chongqing Education Commission (KJZD-K202100603).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: State of health prognostics for series battery packs: A universal deep learning method
Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Che, Z. Deng, P. Li, X. Tang, K. Khosravinia, X. Lin, X. Hu, State of health prognostics for series battery packs: A universal deep learning method, Energy 238 (2022) 121857.

Energy

Energy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An end-to-end neural network framework for state-of-health estimation and remaining useful life prediction of electric vehicle lithium batteries
Renew Sustain Energy Rev

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Li, Z. Zhang, R. Grosu, Z. Deng, J. Hou, Y. Rong, R. Wu, An end-to-end neural network framework for state-of-health estimation and remaining useful life prediction of electric vehicle lithium batteries, Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews 156 (2022) 111843.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. A. Awadallah, B. Venkatesh, Accuracy improvement of soc estimation in lithium-ion batteries, Journal of Energy Storage 6 (2016) 95–104.
